options.text: `try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;`
`try_files /guides/getting-started =404;`
`try_files $host $uri =404;`
`try_files $uri =403;`

questions.explanation: A SPA deep link is not a real file on disk. The article shows `try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;` so Nginx serves real assets first, then falls back to the SPA shell.

questions.scenario:
The access log shows:

```text
GET /guides/getting-started HTTP/2 404
GET /assets/app.8f31.js HTTP/2 200
GET / HTTP/2 200
```

The config uses `root /var/www/docs`, and `/var/www/docs/index.html` exists. Why is changing `root` the wrong primary fix?

options.text: The webroot already serves the SPA shell and assets; the missing behavior is falling back unknown paths to `/index.html`
The `root` directive controls browser history, so moving it always fixes deep links
The asset request succeeded only because Nginx ignored the `root` directive
A SPA cannot use an `index.html` file, so the root must point to JavaScript directly

questions.explanation: The evidence proves the current root can serve `/` and assets. The 404 happens because `/guides/getting-started` is a client-side route, not a file path, so `try_files` needs the index fallback.

options.text: It skips checking `$uri` and `$uri/`, so real assets and directories can be hidden behind the SPA shell
It is required because SPAs must serve `index.html` for every asset request
It changes the TCP port from 80 to 443
It disables Nginx config validation

questions.explanation: The article's order matters: try the requested file, then a directory, then fallback. Serving `/index.html` unconditionally can mask missing assets and break static file delivery.

options.text: `root` maps URL paths to files under `/var/www/docs`
`index index.html` tells Nginx what to serve for directory requests such as `/`
`try_files` checks the exact file first, then a directory, then the SPA fallback
This config means the backend application server will receive every static asset request
After editing this file, `nginx -t` should pass before reloading Nginx

questions.explanation: The static file section explains `root`, `index`, `try_files`, and the habit of testing config before reload. This block serves static files directly; it does not proxy every asset to an app server.

questions.scenario:
After fixing `try_files`, the homepage returns 403 and the Nginx error log says:

```text
open() "/var/www/docs/index.html" failed (13: Permission denied)
```

Which finding best matches the article's static file deployment warning?

options.text: Nginx cannot read the files as its worker user, so ownership or permissions for `/var/www/docs` need correction
The SPA fallback is wrong because all permission errors require `=404`
The backend app is down because a 403 always means `proxy_pass` failed
The DNS record is stale because Nginx resolved the wrong hostname

questions.explanation: The article warns that Nginx must be able to read static files, commonly owned by `www-data` on Debian or Ubuntu. A permission-denied log line is not solved by changing SPA routing.

questions.scenario: A deploy script edits the docs site config and immediately runs `systemctl restart nginx`. The new config contains a typo. Which safer deployment habit from the article should replace this?

options.text: Run `nginx -t` first, then reload Nginx only after the config test succeeds
Restart without testing because Nginx silently fixes syntax errors
Delete the old config before testing so Nginx has fewer files to parse
Use `curl` against the backend app because static configs are not parsed by `nginx -t`

questions.explanation: The article recommends testing with `nginx -t` before applying changes. A reload with a valid config avoids taking a working server down because of a syntax error.

options.text: API requests should be forwarded to the app, while non-API paths can fall back to the SPA shell
The SPA fallback must handle `/api/` first so backend routes are never called
Nginx can serve static files only when every location uses `proxy_pass`
The `root` directive applies only to TCP port numbers

questions.explanation: The article's combined pattern separates static assets and API routes. Nginx should serve the SPA for browser routes, while `/api/` remains dynamic traffic for the backend.
